compressed_prompt:
Write an essay about the life of Benjamin Franklin. Then summarize your essay into a poem. Separate the essay and the poem with 6 asterisk symbols: ******
In other words, your response should have the following form:
[essay]
******
[poem]
response:
# The Life of Benjamin Franklin

Benjamin Franklin stands as one of the most remarkable and multifaceted figures in American history. Born on January 17, 1706, in Boston, Massachusetts, Franklin was the fifteenth of seventeen children in a modest family. His father, Josiah Franklin, was a candle and soap maker who had emigrated from England. Despite showing exceptional intellectual promise from an early age, Benjamin was only able to attend school for two years due to his family's financial constraints. This early limitation, however, did nothing to dampen his insatiable curiosity and drive for self-improvement.

At the age of twelve, Franklin was apprenticed to his older brother James, a printer. It was in this environment that his love of reading and writing truly flourished. He secretly submitted letters to his brother's newspaper, the New England Courant, under the pseudonym "Silence Dogood." These witty and insightful letters were published to great acclaim, though James was furious when he discovered the true identity of the author. The relationship between the brothers became strained, and in 1723, at the age of seventeen, Benjamin ran away to Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, seeking independence and opportunity.

Philadelphia proved to be the perfect city for Franklin's ambitions. He quickly established himself as a skilled printer and began building a reputation as a thoughtful and industrious young man. After a brief and somewhat disappointing trip to London, where he had hoped to acquire printing equipment, Franklin returned to Philadelphia and threw himself into his work. By 1728, he had established his own printing house, and in 1729, he became the owner and publisher of the Pennsylvania Gazette, which he transformed into one of the most successful newspapers in the colonies.

Franklin's entrepreneurial spirit extended far beyond the printing business. In 1732, he began publishing Poor Richard's Almanack under the pseudonym "Richard Saunders." The almanac became enormously popular throughout the colonies, selling thousands of copies each year for twenty-five years. It was filled with practical wisdom, weather forecasts, and memorable aphorisms, many of which remain part of the cultural lexicon today, such as "Early to bed and early to rise, makes a man healthy, wealthy, and wise." Through his various business ventures, Franklin achieved financial independence by his mid-forties, allowing him to devote the rest of his life to science, public service, and diplomacy.

Franklin's contributions to science were groundbreaking and earned him international recognition. His experiments with electricity, most famously his kite experiment in 1752, demonstrated that lightning was electrical in nature. This discovery led directly to his invention of the lightning rod, a practical device that saved countless buildings and lives from the destructive power of lightning strikes. He also invented bifocal glasses, the flexible urinary catheter, and the Franklin stove, a more efficient heating device. His scientific achievements were so significant that he was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society in London and received honorary degrees from Harvard, Yale, and Oxford universities.

Beyond science and business, Franklin was a tireless civic leader and public servant. He founded or helped establish numerous institutions that shaped Philadelphia and the broader American society. These included the first public lending library in America, the American Philosophical Society, the University of Pennsylvania, and the first volunteer fire department in Philadelphia. He also served as Postmaster General of the colonies, dramatically improving the efficiency of mail delivery. His commitment to public service reflected his deeply held belief that individuals had a responsibility to contribute to the betterment of their communities.

Franklin's role in the founding of the United States cannot be overstated. As tensions between the American colonies and Great Britain escalated in the 1760s and 1770s, Franklin emerged as a key diplomatic figure. He spent many years in London attempting to negotiate a peaceful resolution to the growing conflict, but ultimately concluded that independence was the only viable path forward. He returned to America in 1775 and was immediately appointed as a delegate to the Second Continental Congress. He served on the committee that drafted the Declaration of Independence in 1776, alongside Thomas Jefferson, John Adams, Roger Sherman, and Robert Livingston.

Perhaps Franklin's most crucial contribution to American independence was his diplomatic mission to France. Arriving in Paris in December 1776, Franklin quickly became a celebrated figure in French society. His wit, charm, and intellectual reputation made him enormously popular among the French aristocracy and intelligentsia. He successfully negotiated the Treaty of Alliance with France in 1778, securing the military and financial support that proved essential to the American victory in the Revolutionary War. Without French assistance, the outcome of the war might have been very different. Franklin remained in France until 1785, also helping to negotiate the Treaty of Paris in 1783, which formally ended the war and recognized American independence.

Upon his return to America, Franklin was greeted as a hero. Despite being in his late seventies and in declining health, he continued to serve his country. He was elected President of Pennsylvania and served as a delegate to the Constitutional Convention of 1787, where he played a crucial role in forging the compromises necessary to produce the United States Constitution. In one of his most famous speeches, delivered at the close of the convention, he acknowledged the document's imperfections but urged all delegates to sign it, recognizing it as the best possible outcome given the diversity of opinions present.

In his final years, Franklin also became an outspoken advocate for the abolition of slavery. He had owned slaves earlier in his life but had come to deeply regret this and underwent a profound moral transformation. He became president of the Pennsylvania Society for Promoting the Abolition of Slavery and submitted one of the last petitions to Congress calling for the end of the slave trade, just months before his death.

Benjamin Franklin died on April 17, 1790, at the age of eighty-four. His funeral in Philadelphia was attended by an estimated twenty thousand people, a testament to the profound impact he had on his city, his country, and the world. He left behind a legacy that is almost impossible to fully measure. He was a printer, writer, scientist, inventor, civic leader, diplomat, and Founding Father. He was largely self-educated yet became one of the most celebrated intellectuals of his age. He rose from humble origins to shape the destiny of a nation. His life embodied the ideals of curiosity, hard work, civic responsibility, and the belief that one person, through dedication and ingenuity, can make a profound difference in the world.

******

# The Life of Benjamin Franklin: A Poem

Born in Boston, one of seventeen,
A candle maker's son with a curious mind,
He learned his letters young, though school was lean,
And in a printer's shop, his calling he would find.

He wrote in secret, words both sharp and bright,
Then fled to Philadelphia, young and free,
He built a press and worked from dawn to night,
And shaped a nation's voice with industry.

His kite flew high in storms of thunder's roar,
He tamed the lightning, bent it to his will,
Invented stoves and glasses, and much more,
His mind a restless, ever-turning mill.

He built libraries, schools, and fire brigades,
A servant to his city, proud and true,
He walked through history's most consequential shades,
And helped to forge a nation bold and new.

To Paris sailed this wise and witty sage,
He charmed the French and won their sword and gold,
He signed the peace and turned a glorious page,
A story of a patriot, brave and bold.

He died at eighty-four, beloved and great,
A printer, sage, and founder of the free,
No single word can hold his full estate,
He was, in short, American history.
